raw_case:
Axillary skin lesion: A rare presentation of metastatic male breast cancer
Male breast cancer is an uncommon condition, accounting for less than 1% of all breast carcinomas and under 1.5% of all malignant tumors in men. Skin lesions can often be the initial reason for consultation. At this advanced stage, diagnosis is typically delayed, leading to a poor prognosis. Herein, we report the case of a 66-year-old man who presented with a dermo-epidermal axillary mass, indicative of cutaneous metastasis from an invasive ductal carcinoma.

Introduction

Breast cancer in men is a rare disease, accounting for approximately 1% of cancers. Its incidence is too low to warrant systematic screening. In contrast to female breast cancer, male breast cancer often presents with skin lesions that affect the areola, nipple-areolar complex, or the surrounding skin. These cutaneous manifestations can, in some instances, be the primary presenting feature. Breast imaging in men is primarily diagnostic, different techniques such as mammography, ultrasound, and possibly magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) can be used. These methods can help reassure some patients or guide potential biopsy procedures for histological examination [1].We present the case of a 66-year-old man with a dermo-epidermal axillary mass, indicative of cutaneous metastasis from invasive ductal carcinoma. In presenting this case report, we aim to underscore the importance of considering male breast cancer when evaluating skin lesions, in order deliver an accurate diagnosis and provide adequate treatment for patients.

Case report

This case involves a 66-year-old man with no significant medical history who has been experiencing cutaneous swelling and itching in the left axillary region for the past 4 months. Clinical examination identified a subcutaneous mass in the left axillary region. An ultrasound was performed, revealing a well-circumscribed dermo-epidermal mass with regular contours, which appeared hypoechoic and heterogeneous, oriented perpendicular to the skin, and showed peripheral color Doppler uptake (Fig. 1). Additionally, the axillary lymph nodes exhibited a thick cortex and loss of the fatty hilum (Fig. 2).To achieve better characterization, an MRI of the axillary region was requested. This imaging revealed an oval dermo-epidermal mass with regular contours and intermediate signal intensity on both T1 and T2-weighted images. The mass exhibited homogeneous and intense enhancement following contrast injection. It remained distant from the surrounding muscles, notably the serratus anterior, triceps brachii, and latissimus dorsi. Additionally, suspicious axillary lymph nodes were observed (Fig. 3).Initially, a core needle biopsy was performed on the skin mass; however, it was inconclusive on 2 separate occasions. Consequently, a biopsy of the axillary lymph node was made, it confirmed metastatic breast carcinoma, histologically classified as intracanalicular carcinoma. The tumor was negative for estrogen and progesterone receptors but positive for the HER2 receptor.A CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed, revealing multiple solid nodules of varying size secondary to metastatic breast carcinoma (Fig. 4).The patient's case was presented at a multidisciplinary meeting, and a treatment plan involving a combination of the chemotherapy drugs Paclitaxel and Carboplatin, along with dual HER2 blockade, was decided. The patient remains stable, as indicated by the follow-up CT scan conducted 5 months later.

Discussion

Breast cancer accounts for less than 1% of all cancers in men [2,3]. The average age at diagnosis ranges from 60 to 70 years, which is 10 years later than in women [3,4]. The prognosis is bleaker than in the female form because the diagnosis is often made late [1]. The risk factors include heredity (BRCA II), reduced testosterone synthesis, and Klinefelter syndrome. As in women, invasive ductal carcinoma is the most common histological type [1].The clinical symptoms are similar to those in women, with a majority of cases presenting as a well-defined, hard, painless mass, primarily located retroareolarly. Serosanguinous discharge or axillary lymphadenopathy are rarely symptomatic [1]. Unlike female breast cancer, male breast cancer is characterized by the frequency of skin lesions affecting the areola, nipple-areolar complex, or the entire skin. These cutaneous manifestations can, in some cases, be the main presenting feature, as in our case [1].Breast imaging in men is predominantly diagnostic, as the low incidence of male breast cancer does not justify routine screening [1]. Different imaging techniques such as mammography, ultrasound, and MRI can reassure some patients or guide potential biopsy sampling for histological examination [1].The use of mammography in men is possible and should be bilateral, even if the symptoms are unilateral. When technically challenging, only an oblique view is obtained [1,5]. In 60% of cases, breast cancer presents as a mass with microlobulated, spiculated, or ill-defined contours, typically located retroareolarly and often eccentric to the nipple [6]. Microcalcifications are rarely a prominent feature due to the low incidence of isolated ductal carcinoma in situ [7]. Associated anomalies classically described in female breast cancer, such as axillary lymphadenopathy, skin retraction, and nipple retraction, are also found. In cases of discharge, exploration via galactography is technically feasible. Cancer is bilateral in 1% to 4% of cases, justifying bilateral mammography even if the symptoms are unilateral [8].On ultrasound, male breast cancer presents as an irregular hypoechoic cavity with variable attenuation associated with a peripheral halo [9]. Posterior acoustic shadowing is inconsistent, as the frequent retroareolar location of the tumor makes this sign nonspecific. Cancer can also manifest as a cystic lesion, with a mural nodule indicative of papillary ductal carcinoma in situ. Additionally, in men, the discovery of a cyst on ultrasound, regardless of its appearance, should prompt cytological and/or histological sampling. Ultrasound is particularly useful for exploring deep masses that may be missed on mammography. Upon axillary exploration, lymphadenopathy is detected in 50% of breast cancers [10].The performance of breast MRI in men is possible, even though there is no clearly established consensus indication. The radiological appearance is that of an irregular mass with intense and early enhancement with secondary washout according to a type 3 curve. The Breast Imaging Reporting And Data System (BIRADS) MRI criteria are applicable to breast cancer in men [11].

Conclusion

Male breast cancer is a rare disease, representing about 1% of all cancers. Cutaneous lesions may be the initial reason for consultation, often leading to delayed diagnosis and a poor prognosis. Imaging is essential in managing these patients, guiding biopsy procedures, determining locoregional and distant staging, and monitoring post-treatment progress.

Patient consent

The authors of this manuscript declare that an informed consent for publication of this case was obtained from the patient.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information:
  - Age: 66 years
  - Gender: Male
  - Relevant Medical History: None noted
- Chief Complaint:
  - Cutaneous swelling and itching in the left axillary region for the past 4 months.
- History of Present Illness:
  - Duration: 4 months
  - Symptoms: Left axillary dermo-epidermal mass coupled with cutaneous swelling and itching.
- Past Medical History:
  - None reported.
- Personal History:
  - Not specified.
- Family History:
  - None reported.
- Physical Examination:
  - Subcutaneous mass in the left axillary region.
- Ancillary Tests:
  - Ultrasound: Revealed a dermo-epidermal mass with regular contours, hypoechoic and heterogeneous characteristics, peripheral Doppler uptake, and suspicious axillary lymph nodes (thick cortex, loss of fatty hilum).
  - MRI: Showed an oval dermo-epidermal mass distanced from adjacent muscles and suspicious axillary lymph nodes.
  - Biopsy:
    - Initial: Two core needle biopsies of the skin mass were inconclusive.
    - Axillary lymph node biopsy: Confirmed metastatic breast carcinoma, histologically classified as intracanalicular carcinoma (HER2-positive, estrogen and progesterone receptor-negative).
  - CT Scan: Identified multiple solid nodules, indicative of metastatic spread, in the chest, abdomen, and pelvis.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations Based on Presentation**:
   - The axillary subcutaneous mass raised concerns for malignancy with possible axillary lymph node involvement. Differential diagnoses included:
     - Primary breast cancer (male or female).
     - Axillary lymphadenopathy due to lymphoma or other metastatic cancers.
     - Infectious or inflammatory conditions.
  
2. **Ruling Out Potential Diagnoses**:
   - **Infectious or Inflammatory Process**:
     - No evidence of systemic symptoms (fever, constitutional signs) or infection present via the patient's history or imaging.
     - Excluded based on the chronicity of symptoms and imaging findings consistent with malignancy.
   - **Lymphoma**:
     - The imaging and biopsy findings were not consistent with lymphoma (no hallmark Reed-Sternberg cells or typical lymphoma characteristics on imaging).

3. **Focus on Breast Cancer**:
   - Male breast cancer, though rare, was considered due to imaging findings (axillary mass with suspicious lymphadenopathy) and eventual histological confirmation of intracanalicular carcinoma. The HER2-positive profile further supported this diagnosis.
   - Imaging findings of metastases on CT ruled out a primary localized breast cancer and confirmed advanced metastatic disease.

4. **Confirmation of Final Diagnosis**:
   - Histological evidence from biopsy coupled with imaging findings led to the final diagnosis of metastatic male breast cancer, specifically invasive ductal carcinoma with cutaneous metastases.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
Metastatic male breast cancer (invasive ductal carcinoma with cutaneous metastasis, HER2-positive).

---

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by reviewing the case. The patient is a 66-year-old male with a 4-month history of a left axillary mass, itching, and swelling. The initial tests showed a dermo-epidermal mass and suspicious lymph nodes. The core needle biopsies of the skin mass were inconclusive, but the axillary lymph node biopsy came back as metastatic breast carcinoma, specifically intracanalicular carcinoma. The tumor is HER2-positive and hormone receptor-negative. The CT scan also shows multiple nodules in the chest, abdomen, and pelvis, indicating metastasis.

First, I need to consider the primary source of the metastatic carcinoma. Breast cancer in males is rare but possible. Intracanalicular carcinoma is a subtype of invasive ductal carcinoma. Since the lymph node biopsy confirmed this, even though the skin mass biopsies were inconclusive, the primary might still be in the breast. But wait, the patient is male, and male breast cancer typically presents as a retroareolar mass. However, there's no mention of a breast lump here. The presentation here is an axillary mass. Maybe the primary is occult, and the axillary metastasis is the first sign. Alternatively, could the skin mass be the primary site? But the lymph node biopsy shows metastatic breast cancer, which usually originates from breast tissue.

Next, the HER2-positive status is important. HER2-positive cancers are more aggressive but have targeted therapies. The estrogen and progesterone receptor-negative status is less common in male breast cancer, which usually is hormone receptor-positive. But this case is ER/PR negative. That might suggest a different biology, perhaps more similar to some female breast cancers.

The CT scan showing multiple nodules in various regions points to stage IV metastatic disease. The primary site needs to be confirmed. Since the lymph node biopsy is breast carcinoma, the primary is likely in the breast. But in males, breast exams are often overlooked. The patient might have a small primary tumor that wasn't palpable or noticed. Alternatively, could this be a metastasis from another site misclassified? Intracanalicular carcinoma is a breast cancer subtype, so that's unlikely. The term "intracanalicular" might be a translation error or a less common term; perhaps it refers to invasive ductal carcinoma with specific features.

So, even though the skin mass was biopsied and was inconclusive, the lymph node's biopsy showing metastatic breast carcinoma is key. The primary tumor might be in the breast, possibly undetected. Male breast cancer can present with axillary lymphadenopathy as the first sign. Therefore, the final diagnosis would be metastatic HER2-positive breast carcinoma, likely originating from an occult primary in the breast, with metastases to the axillary lymph nodes and distant sites (chest, abdomen, pelvis). The cutaneous swelling in the axilla could be a metastatic skin lesion from the breast primary.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient’s axillary lymph node biopsy confirmed metastatic breast carcinoma (intracanalicular subtype), which is a rare but documented variant of invasive ductal carcinoma. Despite inconclusive skin mass biopsies, the lymph node findings are definitive for breast origin.  
<step 2> Male breast cancer, though uncommon, can present with axillary lymphadenopathy as the initial manifestation. The absence of a reported breast mass does not exclude a primary breast tumor, as occult primaries can occur, particularly in males with limited clinical breast evaluation.  
<step 3> The HER2-positive, ER/PR-negative profile aligns with aggressive tumor biology and supports breast origin, as HER2-positive carcinomas are well-characterized in breast cancer. The receptor negativity is atypical for male breast cancer but not impossible.  
<step 4> The CT findings of widespread metastatic nodules correlate with advanced metastatic breast carcinoma. The axillary cutaneous swelling likely represents direct extension or cutaneous metastasis from the primary breast malignancy.  

### Answer:
Metastatic HER2-positive invasive ductal carcinoma (intracanalicular subtype) of the breast with axillary lymph node and distant metastases (Stage IV).

reasoning_eval.step: The patient’s axillary lymph node biopsy confirmed metastatic breast carcinoma (intracanalicular subtype), which is a rare but documented variant of invasive ductal carcinoma. Despite inconclusive skin mass biopsies, the lymph node findings are definitive for breast origin.

Male breast cancer, though uncommon, can present with axillary lymphadenopathy as the initial manifestation. The absence of a reported breast mass does not exclude a primary breast tumor, as occult primaries can occur, particularly in males with limited clinical breast evaluation.

The HER2-positive, ER/PR-negative profile aligns with aggressive tumor biology and supports breast origin, as HER2-positive carcinomas are well-characterized in breast cancer. The receptor negativity is atypical for male breast cancer but not impossible.

The CT findings of widespread metastatic nodules correlate with advanced metastatic breast carcinoma. The axillary cutaneous swelling likely represents direct extension or cutaneous metastasis from the primary breast malignancy.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> The axillary subcutaneous mass raised concerns for malignancy with possible axillary lymph node involvement; differential diagnoses included primary breast cancer (male or female), axillary lymphadenopathy due to lymphoma or other metastatic cancers, and infectious or inflammatory conditions.

<Step 2> No evidence of an infectious or inflammatory process was found, as there were no systemic symptoms or infection present, and the chronicity of symptoms and imaging findings were more consistent with malignancy.
<Step 3> Lymphoma was excluded based on imaging and biopsy findings, which did not show hallmark Reed-Sternberg cells or typical lymphoma characteristics.

<Step 4> Male breast cancer was considered due to imaging findings (axillary mass with suspicious lymphadenopathy) and confirmed by histological evidence of intracanalicular carcinoma. The HER2-positive profile further supported this diagnosis.
<Step 5> Metastatic disease was confirmed by imaging findings showing metastases on CT, ruling out a primary localized breast cancer.
<Step 6> The final diagnosis was metastatic male breast cancer, specifically invasive ductal carcinoma with cutaneous metastases, confirmed by histological evidence from biopsy and imaging findings.
Final diagnosis: Metastatic male breast cancer (invasive ductal carcinoma with cutaneous metastasis, HER2-positive).
